turns.content: Hey, I'm having trouble understanding the difference between #[wasm_bindgen] and web-sys when I'm trying to call a JavaScript function from my Rust WASM module. Which one should I actually use?

Hey! Great question. The #[wasm_bindgen] macro is the core entry point that tells wasm-bindgen how to bridge your Rust code with JavaScript, while web-sys provides a set of types that mirror browser APIs like DOM elements or Window objects for type safety. You absolutely need #[wasm_bindgen] on every function you want to expose to JS, but you typically use web-sys inside your Rust code if you're accessing browser features directly without going through JavaScript first.
Okay, so I have a function that needs to return a complex object from JS. Should I use JsValue or just regular types like Vec<String>?

If you're returning arbitrary JavaScript objects or values that don't map cleanly to Rust primitives, you should wrap them in JsValue using #[wasm_bindgen] with the appropriate attributes. However, if your return type is something like Vec<String> or a simple struct that implements Serialize, you can usually just use those native Rust types directly and wasm-bindgen will handle the conversion automatically.
I'm getting an error saying I can't return my struct. It seems like I need to manually construct JsValue instances with values()

That's a common mistake when you're first diving into this. You don't necessarily need to manually create JsValue instances unless your Rust type doesn't implement the necessary traits or isn't directly convertible. If your struct has a Serialize implementation, wasm-bindgen should handle it for you. The error suggests you might be trying to return something that can't be serialized yet.
Wait, I think I misunderstood how the conversion works. Can't I just use JsValue::from_serde() on my struct and then wrap it in a #[wasm_bindgen] function?

You're very close! JsValue::from_serde is useful for converting arbitrary serde values to JavaScript, but you don't typically use it inside the #[wasm_bindgen] macro itself. Instead, you'd implement Serialize on your Rust struct and let wasm-bindgen do the heavy lifting of conversion when you return that struct from a function.
Ah okay, so I should just add #[serde] to my struct? But what about bundling this with wasm-pack? Does it handle all these conversions automatically?

wasm-pack will bundle your project and generate the necessary JavaScript glue code, but you still need to manually add #[serde] attributes to your Rust structs if they contain complex data. wasm-pack doesn't magically convert everything for you—you do need to tell it what types are serializable.
Got it, so the workflow is: define struct with #[serde], add #[wasm_bindgen] to function returning that struct, and wasm-pack handles the rest?

Exactly! That's the standard pattern. Define your Rust types with proper serde attributes, expose them through #[wasm_bindgen] functions, then run wasm-pack build to generate the JavaScript bindings and bundle everything together.
One last thing—what if I need to call a specific JS function that returns an object? Do I use web-sys::HTMLElement or just #[wasm_bindgen]?

For calling arbitrary JavaScript functions from Rust, you should use #[wasm_bindgen] with the js_name attribute to specify which function in your JS file it corresponds to. If you need types like HTMLElement or Window for your Rust code logic, those come from web-sys and you'd import them at the top of your module.

I'm trying to get back into Rust after five years and I'm stuck on a code review. My colleague wrote some WASM bindings using #[wasm_bindgen] but they're returning JsValue instead of concrete types like String or i32, which makes it hard to type-check later.

That is actually a very common mistake when first diving into wasm-bindgen. When you return JsValue directly from a #[wasm_bindgen] function without specifying the concrete Rust type, the caller has to do manual parsing and unwrapping of the JsValue enum variants before they can safely use the data.

Exactly. They have me manually inspecting JsValue::from_str or checking if it's a String variant every time I call their function, which feels like an anti-pattern compared to standard Rust return types.

You are right that this defeats the purpose of safe FFI. The wasm-bindgen crate is designed so that #[wasm_bindgen] functions can declare explicit output types like String or i32, which allows Rust to automatically handle the conversion from JsValue via web-sys and provide proper type safety without manual inspection.
So if I change their return statement to use concrete types, does wasm-pack still bundle it correctly?

Yes, wasm-pack will bundle it correctly because the #[wasm_bindgen] macro generates the necessary glue code for those specific output types. However, you should also check if they are using JsCast to convert values when calling into JS functions from Rust, since web-sys often requires explicit casting when bridging direction changes.

Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to call a JavaScript function from Rust but it feels like magic. The docs say #[wasm_bindgen] and JsValue but I can't figure out how the types actually map.

It's not really magic; wasm-bindgen generates the glue code at compile time using macros to create FFI boundaries. When you annotate a function with #[wasm_bindgen], it inserts runtime logic that handles type conversion and memory management so Rust can call into JS without manual pointer manipulation.

Okay, but I'm getting an error about JsValue. It seems like my function is expecting a specific return type but the macro is giving me something else. Is this a limitation of how wasm-pack bundles things?

That's not a bundling issue; it's about what you're declaring in your Rust function signature. JsValue is the generic wrapper for any JS value, but if your JS function returns a string or number, wasm-bindgen can infer that and give you more specific types like &str or f64 instead of forcing you to handle raw JsValue manually.
I see. So I should be avoiding #[wasm_bindgen] for everything? Or is there a time when I really need the generic wrapper?

You only use JsValue directly when you're dealing with polymorphic JS values that can't be statically inferred, like dynamic arrays or objects. For most cases, wasm-bindgen's type inference on functions is sufficient and safer because it prevents runtime panics from bad type conversions.
Got it. But what if I need to pass a complex Rust struct with nested fields? Does the macro handle that automatically or do I have to write extra glue code?

The #[wasm_bindgen] attribute handles most of the serialization for you, but if your struct has non-serializable fields like untracked pointers, you'll need to mark those with #[cfg_attr(target_arch = "wasm32", derive(Debug))] or use explicit conversions. wasm-pack's default config usually works fine unless you're doing something very low-level.
I'm still confused about the memory management part. When I call a JS function, does Rust actually allocate heap memory for return values or is it all just references?

wasm-bindgen uses reference semantics by default; it doesn't automatically allocate heap memory for simple types unless you explicitly use JsValue::new(). For primitive conversions, the runtime creates temporary objects on the JS side and returns a reference back to Rust without extra allocations.
So performance-wise, is there overhead compared to writing raw C FFI bindings manually?

Yes, wasm-bindgen has a small runtime cost because it needs to check types and manage the JS->Rust boundary at every call site. But for most applications this overhead is negligible compared to the complexity of writing manual C FFI bindings in Rust.
Alright, one last thing. If I want to expose a large array from JS back to Rust, does wasm-bindgen support that or do I need to write custom code?

You can use JsValue::from_vec() to pass vectors directly, but for large arrays you might want to consider using web-sys's TypedArray bindings or implementing a custom conversion trait. wasm-pack handles basic serialization well, but large data structures often benefit from explicit type annotations.

Hey, I've read about #[wasm_bindgen] and JsValue before but when I try to call a JavaScript function from Rust it always returns null or the wrong type. I keep thinking I just need to add #[wasm_bindgen] to my function signature like normal Rust code.

You've hit the classic beginner trap here. The #[wasm_bindgen] attribute doesn't automatically make your function behave like a JS callback; you still need to explicitly return the correct type from Rust side so wasm-bindgen knows how to map it back to JavaScript.
So even if I have #[wasm_bindgen] on my function, I can't just use i32 or String directly? Why does everything return JsValue instead?

Exactly. When you call a JS function from Rust through wasm-bindgen, the result always comes back as JsValue because that's the universal container type for any JavaScript value in FFI. You can't just return i32 or String directly unless you're exporting a Rust function to JS, not calling JS functions.
Okay so I need to convert JsValue back to my actual type? And what about web-sys for DOM elements?

You do need to convert it using the From trait or specific conversion functions like js_sys::JsValue into your Rust types. For DOM elements you'd use web-sys which provides safe wrappers around DOM objects so you don't have to deal with raw JsValue for things like HTMLElement.
That makes sense, but what about bundling? Does wasm-pack handle these conversions automatically?

wasm-pack will bundle your Rust code with the necessary JS glue layer that handles the FFI boundary, but it won't magically fix type mismatches in your Rust code. You still need to write #[wasm_bindgen] correctly and use JsValue properly for cross-language calls.
Ah okay so I should just make sure my Rust function returns the right type before calling it from JS?

Not exactly backwards. When you call a JS function from Rust, your Rust function receives JsValue as its return value and you must convert it to your expected Rust type. Think of it like this: imagine JS sends you a generic envelope (JsValue) containing something specific, and you need to open it and extract the actual letter before passing it to your own logic.
So the analogy is JsValue is like an opaque wrapper that hides what's inside until I explicitly unwrap it with the right trait?

Perfect, you got it. JsValue acts as a universal container that lets any JS value cross the boundary into Rust, but you have to consciously choose which conversion function to use based on what's actually inside.
What's the difference between using cbindgen for C bindings and how we use #[wasm_bindgen] attributes in Rust?

The difference is that cbindgen generates C code from your Rust source, whereas #[wasm_bindgen] lets you directly expose Rust functions to JavaScript. You can think of it like this: cbindgen builds a bridge by constructing a separate roadway in another language, while #[wasm_bindgen] builds the bridge directly into your existing structure so traffic flows immediately without needing construction crews to lay down new asphalt.
I feel like I'm just copying boilerplate code everywhere. How does wasm-bindgen actually handle the conversion between Rust types and JavaScript values?

That's a common frustration, but wasm-bindgen handles this through a special trait called JsValue which acts as the universal container for any JS value. When you write #[wasm_bindgen] on a function, it automatically wraps your Rust arguments into JsValue variants like JsString or JsNumber depending on what data type you pass in.
So I don't need to manually cast everything? What if I want to return complex objects from my function?

You don't need manual casts because wasm-bindgen uses a macro that inspects your Rust code and generates the necessary glue code. For returning complex objects, you can use #[wasm_bindgen] with a return type like JsValue or wrap a struct in a custom class definition. It's like having an automatic translator at every door instead of asking someone to translate manually.
What about web-sys? Do I need those for this to work?

web-sys is a collection of types that represent the Web API, like Window or Document. You don't strictly need it for basic #[wasm_bindgen] function calls, but if you want to access DOM elements directly from your Rust code, web-sys provides those bindings so you can interact with the browser just like normal Rust.
Can I bundle this with wasm-pack and expect it to work out of the box?

Yes, you can absolutely bundle this with wasm-pack. Just add your crate to a new project or an existing one, run wasm-pack build --target web, and it will generate the necessary WebAssembly file along with JavaScript glue code that handles the interop.
I'm still worried about memory safety. What happens if I accidentally pass a reference to a JS object?

That's exactly why wasm-bindgen is designed this way; it enforces strict type checking at the boundary. If you try to pass an invalid JsValue variant or mismatched types, the build will fail before your code even runs. It's like having a security guard at every checkpoint who won't let you through if your ID doesn't match.
Is there anything specific about how wasm-pack handles dependencies compared to cargo?

wasm-pack builds on top of cargo but adds extra steps for WebAssembly optimization. It runs a separate build process that strips out unused code and generates the final .wasm file, while cargo just handles the Rust compilation itself.
Can I see an example of how to call a JS function from my Rust code using this?

Sure, here is the pattern you would use: #[wasm_bindgen] allows you to write pub fn my_function(js_string: JsString) -> JsValue; and then call it from JS like my_function("hello"). The wasm-bindgen macro translates that into a function that accepts JsString and returns JsValue.
Does this approach scale well for large projects or is it just good for small tools?

It scales very well because the overhead of wasm-bindgen is minimal. Once you set up your build pipeline with wasm-pack, adding more Rust libraries to expose to JS is as easy as writing a new #[wasm_bindgen] function and rebuilding.
I have a Rust library that needs to run in the browser, but I'm confused about how to make it talk to JavaScript. Should I use FFI or something else?

For browser environments, you should absolutely use FFI via wasm-bindgen rather than C-style FFI. Think of wasm-bindgen as a bridge that translates between Rust and JavaScript automatically without needing manual memory management or type casting tables like traditional FFI requires. Instead of writing raw pointers to the heap, you just define #[wasm_bindgen] attributes on your functions so they become callable from JS.
Wait, I thought FFI was only for C interoperability? Can't Rust use it with JavaScript too?

That is a common misconception. While traditional FFI usually refers to calling C code from other languages using inline assembly or explicit type conversion tables, wasm-bindgen provides an idiomatic way to do this in the WebAssembly ecosystem without needing those low-level tricks. It handles the marshaling of Rust types into JavaScript objects and vice versa through traits like JsValue, so you don't need to manually implement a C-style FFI interface for JS.
Okay, but what if I want to call specific JS functions back into my Rust code? Do I need web-sys or something similar?

Yes, when you need to access browser APIs like DOM elements or the window object from your Rust side, you use web-sys. You can call a function defined on a trait like wasm_bindgen::JsCast to cast a JsValue into a concrete type like web_sys::HtmlElement if needed. This lets you seamlessly invoke JavaScript methods that modify the page while your Rust code handles the logic.
I see, so I don't need to bundle my Rust crate manually? Just put it in the project?

Not exactly; for browser deployments you should use wasm-pack to build your Rust library into a WebAssembly module and JavaScript glue code. It handles bundling the Rust binary with the necessary dependencies like wasm-bindgen and web-sys, then generates an index.js file that exports functions wrapped in #[wasm_bindgen] so they can be imported by frontend code.
Got it, so I just need to ensure my Rust types implement the right traits for serialization? What about JsValue?

JsValue is the generic container type that wasm-bindgen uses to represent any JavaScript value in your Rust code. When you return a complex object from Rust into JS, you typically wrap it in JsValue or use traits like serde_json::Value if you need JSON serialization before passing it across the boundary.
I've been using a manual C interop setup for my Rust library but it's getting messy, is there a better way to bridge Rust and WebAssembly today?

You should definitely consider wasm-bindgen instead of manual bindings. It lets you write #[wasm_bindgen] attributes on your functions so they automatically get exposed as JavaScript, avoiding all that manual C header work.
That sounds nice but how do I actually call a JS function back into my Rust code without dealing with raw pointers?

You use JsValue to represent arbitrary JavaScript values and pass them through your FFI boundaries. For example, you can take &JsValue as an argument or return a new JsValue so the browser can access complex objects like arrays or maps from Rust.
What about calling standard web APIs like DOM manipulation? Do I need to import anything extra?

Yes, you'd typically use the web-sys crate which provides typed wrappers for those web APIs. It makes calling things like document.getElementById much safer than working with raw JsValue or untyped JS.
I'm still confused about how to actually bundle this into a browser environment, what's the recommended tooling?

You should use wasm-pack for bundling. It handles the conversion of your Rust crate into WebAssembly and generates the necessary JS glue code so you can drop it directly into your HTML files or Node.js projects.
